And how?","opinion":"As a 63-year-old mechanic from Rome, I've spent my life working with my hands, fixing engines and machines, and I don't know much about fancy terms like 'stereotype threat in STEM.' From what I gather, it's when people feel pressured or scared because of negative ideas about their group, like women or minorities being told they're not good at science or math. I've seen similar things in my own life—maybe not in schools or labs, but in the garage, where some folks assume an older guy like me can't keep up with new technology. It's frustrating, and it can make you doubt yourself, even if you've been doing the job for decades. I think we should address it because it's unfair and holds people back. In my view, based on my experience as a part-time worker with only primary school education but parents who were more educated, everyone deserves a chance to learn and contribute without these extra burdens. How to address it? Well, from my perspective, it starts with simple respect and encouragement. In my shop, I try to treat everyone equally, whether they're young or old, man or woman, and focus on their skills, not stereotypes. Schools and workplaces could do the same—create environments where people feel supported, not judged. Maybe offer more hands-on training and mentorship, like how I learned from older mechanics when I was starting out. Since I'm not very social and spend little time on social media, I believe in face-to-face interactions and practical solutions. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion: As a 63-year-old mechanic from Rome, I've spent my life working with my hands, fixing engines and machines, and I don't know much about fancy terms like 'stereotype threat in STEM.' From what I gather, it's when people feel pressured or scared because of negative ideas about their group, like women or minorities being told they're not good at science or math. I've seen similar things in my own life—maybe not in schools or labs, but in the garage, where some folks assume an older guy like me can't keep up with new technology. It's frustrating, and it can make you doubt yourself, even if you've been doing the job for decades. I think we should address it because it's unfair and holds people back. In my view, based on my experience as a part-time worker with only primary school education but parents who were more educated, everyone deserves a chance to learn and contribute without these extra burdens. How to address it? Well, from my perspective, it starts with simple respect and encouragement. In my shop, I try to treat everyone equally, whether they're young or old, man or woman, and focus on their skills, not stereotypes. Schools and workplaces could do the same—create environments where people feel supported, not judged. Maybe offer more hands-on training and mentorship, like how I learned from older mechanics when I was starting out. Since I'm not very social and spend little time on social media, I believe in face-to-face interactions and practical solutions. Addressing stereotype threat isn't about big theories; it's about making sure no one feels excluded or underestimated, so they can do their best work, just like I try to do every day, even when I'm feeling down.
